content.text:
Data Security
Semester:
3  |
Type:
UEF31  |
VHS:
63h  |
Weekly hours:
4h30  |
Lecture:
1h30  |
Tutorial:
1h30  |
Lab:
1h30
Personal work VHS:
42h  |
Coefficient:
3  |
Credits:
5
Teaching Objectives
This course introduces the main concepts of data security, highlighting the fundamental pillars of confidentiality, integrity, and availability. Students will be introduced to identification and authentication techniques, then study different access control policies. Cryptographic concepts will be explored, covering the main algorithms used in encryption, authentication, and electronic signatures — from history to modern applications, including symmetric encryption (DES, 3DES, AES), asymmetric cryptography (RSA), and electronic signature techniques.
Course Content (21h)
Chapter 1: Introduction
CIA fundamentals of data security: Confidentiality, Integrity, Availability
Security vs privacy
Risks associated with data manipulation and analysis
Identification and Authentication: techniques (2FA, MFA, certificate...)
Chapter 3: Access Control
Definition and objectives
Access control policies (ACLs, DAC, MAC, RBAC, ABAC...)
Chapter 4: Cryptography
Historical background
Classical cryptography: mono and polyalphabetic
Modern cryptography:
Secret-key cryptography: stream cipher, block cipher, transposition/substitution, Feistel schemes, DES, 3DES, AES
Public-key cryptography: RSA
Cryptanalysis: brute-force attacks, replay attacks, ciphertext-only, chosen-plaintext and chosen-ciphertext attacks, frequency analysis (Al Kindi)
One-way functions, hash functions, key exchange algorithms, zero-knowledge algorithms, quantum encryption, elliptic curves
Chapter 5: Data Security in Transit and at Rest
Encryption of data in transit (HTTPS, SSH)
Encryption of data at rest (hard drives, databases)
Data masking and anonymisation techniques
Securing cloud computing environments
Chapter 6: Database Security
Securing relational databases (SQL Injection, encryption)
NoSQL database security (MongoDB, Cassandra)
Database monitoring and audit
Privilege and access management for databases
Chapter 7: Access Control and Authentication
Access control models (RBAC, ABAC, DAC, MAC)
Authentication methods (2FA, MFA, biometrics)
Identity and Access Management (IAM)
SSO (Single Sign-On) and identity federation
Chapter 8: System and Application Security
Operating system security (Windows, Linux, MacOS)
Web application security (OWASP Top 10, CSP, SameSite Cookies)
API security (OAuth, OpenID Connect)
DevSecOps and security integration in the SDLC
Chapter 9: Network Security
Network and transport layer security (firewalls, IDS/IPS)
Wireless network security (WPA3, WEP, WPA2)
VPN and secure communications
Protocol security (HTTP, HTTPS, DNSSEC)
Assessment
Final examination (in person): 60%
Continuous assessment (CC): 40% — written tests, personal work
Bibliography
W. Stallings (2011).
Computer Security: Principles and Practice
. Prentice Hall.
M. T. Goodrich, R. Tamassia (2010).
Introduction to Computer Security
. Pearson, International Edition.

Identities and authentication
. Foresight Directorate.
HU, V. C., FERRAIOLO, D., KUHN, D. R. et al. (2006).
Assessment of access control systems
. NIST.

Cryptographie et sécurité des systèmes et réseaux
. Hermès - Lavoisier.
W. Stallings (2010).
Cryptography and Network Security: Principles and Practice
. Pearson.
G. Avoine, P. Junod, P. Oechslin (2010).
Sécurité Informatique
. Editions Vuibert.
